LLOYD M. HARMON, JR.
COUNTY COUNSEL

ANTHONY ALBERS
DIANE BARDSLEY
CHIEF DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

County of San Diego
OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL
355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
1600 PACIFIC HIGHWAY, SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-2469
(619) 531-4860

DEPUTIES

ARNE HANSEN'S
LEROY HEILBRON
RALPH E. CALDWELL
WILLIAM D. CHENEY
WILLIAM D. SMITH
WILLIAM W. TAYLOR
JAMES E. ARNOLD
EUGENE R. CONDON
JACQUELINE M. STERN
EUGENE B. STONER
IAN FAIS
LEONARD W. PHILLIPS
DAVID C. PIERSON
THOMAS E. MONTGOMERY
EDMUND E. McCLAY
ROBERT C. SHAW
DONALD MCPHEE
GEORGE W. BREWSTER JR.

TERENCE G. DUTTON
BEVERLY G. FELAND
RONALD J. SALAZAR
LAUREL GALLACHER-TOGAR
CHERYL L. CARTER
PAULINE ROBINSON
PAMELA I. JONES
ANDREW NISHIKAWA
THOMAS TRACY
DAVID B. FLORANCE
GARY L. DAVIES
DEBORAH M. PETERSON
THEODORE FRIEDMAN
CLAUDIA M. FITZPATRICK
R. WADE BECKLEY
PATRICIA A. MEYERS
PALMER E. WRIGHT
LAUREL D. ROMANO
SUSAN D. MAGSAY

June 24, 1992

Ms. Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division,
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Stecher:

Re: Request for Informal Assistance
Your File No. I-92-84

We wish to thank you for giving us an opportunity to provide
you with additional information in respect to your analysis of
the applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974, as
amended, ("the Act") to planning and sponsor groups in San Diego
County. We wish to take issue with that portion of your
conclusion where you state: "It also appears that the members of
the planning and sponsor groups are public officials, who have
disclosure and disqualification requirements under the Act."

As you pointed out in your opinion, members of planning or
sponsor groups would only constitute public officials if the
groups meet the criteria set forth in section 18700(a)(1) of the
Fair Political Practice Commission (FPPC) Regulations which reads
as follows:

"(a) 'Public official at any level of state or local
government' means every natural person who is a member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency.

"(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited
to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or
commissions with decision-making authority. A board or
commission possesses decision-making authority
whenever:

Ms. Stecher                    -2-                    June 24, 1992


                    "(A) It may make a final governmental
            decision;

                    "(B) It may compel a governmental decision;
            or it may prevent a governmental decision either
            by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the
            decision or by reason of a veto which may not be
            overridden; or

                    "(C) It makes substantive recommendations
            which are, and over an extended period of time
            have been, regularly approved without significant
            amendment or modification by another public
            official or governmental agency."  (2 Calif. Code
            of Regs. § 18700(a)(1).)

     Although you indicate that you do not have enough
information to conclude definitively that planning and sponsor
groups meet any of the requirements of section 18700(a)(1), you
assert that "it seems likely that they would meet the
requirements of either subdivisions (a)(1)(B) or (a)(1)(C)...."

     There is nothing in San Diego Board of Supervisors Policy I-
1, a copy of which is attached hereto, which allows planning or
sponsor groups to compel a decision or prevent a decision either
by an exclusive power to initiate or by a veto power which cannot
be overridden. Although the County makes every effort to include
community representatives from the planning and sponsor groups in
the decision-making process, it is clear that the groups are
advisory only and that they do not have the power to compel a
particular decision or to block a particular decis­ion. Even
though the groups may be active and influential in the planning
process, their power is limited to making advisory
recommendations. It seems clear to us that planning and sponsor
groups do not meet the criteria set forth in section
18700(a)(1)(B) of the FPPC Regulations.

     We also question whether planning and sponsor groups have
their substantive recommendations, over an extended period of
time, regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or government agency.
Mr. John G. McLean, Counsel for the FPPC Enforcement Division, in
a letter dated April 24, 1991, a copy of which is attached
hereto, concluded that the Valley Center Community Planning Group
(VCCPG) "recommendations have been regularly amended or
modified", and, "VCCPG members are not subject to the Act's
disclosure or disqualification provisions. (Section 82048 and
Regulation 18700(a)(1)(C).)"

Ms. Stecher                          -3-                          June 24, 1992


     It seems to us that Mr. McLean's conclusion that the Act
does not apply to the VCCPG is in direct conflict with your
conclusion that the Act appears to apply to planning and sponsor
group members. We suggest that the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act only apply to members of
those planning and sponsor groups, if any, which have their
substantive recommendations regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification over an extended period of
time. To decide whether a particular planning or sponsor group
meets this criteria, it is necessary to review the group's
substantive recommendations and then determine factually whether
such recommendations have been regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification over an extended period of
time. Any conclusion regarding the applicability of the Act to a
particular planning or sponsor group which is formulated without
an examination of the relevant facts is of doubtful validity.

     We also note that disclosure and disqualification
requirements similar to those contained in the Act have been made
applicable to planning and sponsor groups by Board of Supervisors
Policy I-1. The Board has recognized that the advisory groups
play a vital and highly visible role in the planning process, and
even though members of a given planning or sponsor group may not
be public officials pursuant to the criteria set forth in section
18700(a)(1) of the FPPC Regulations, the members will be subject
to locally imposed disclosure and disqualification requirements.

     Please reconsider your opinion in light of the above
comments and in light of the contrary conclusions reached by the
counsel for the FPPC Enforcement Division.

                                  Very truly yours,

                                  LLOYD M. HARMON, JR., County Counsel

                                  By    [signature]
                                       ARNE HANSEN, Deputy County Counsel

cc:  San Diego County Grand Jury

Grand Jury
COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO
1420 KETTNER BLVD. SUITE 310
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101-2432
(619) 236-2675

January 23, 1992

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95812

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

The 1991-92 San Diego County Grand Jury hereby asks the Fair
Political Practices Commission the following question:

Are the members of the Planning and Sponsor Groups of the
County of San Diego "public officials" within the meaning
of Government Code Section 87100 by virtue of their
membership in such groups?

It is noted that the Board of Supervisors of San Diego County
in its Policy I-1 and I-1A which cover the formation, purposes and
functions of the Planning and Sponsor Groups has specified that
"planning group members are not county officials." (page 7 of
enclosed Policy I-1)

The Grand Jury's concern is whether or not the Board's
specification that the members of the groups are not public
officials is in conformity with the California Government Code
cited above. Do the cited Government Code sections provide basis
for the Jury to recommend that the Board of Supervisors change its
Policy to specify that Planning and Sponsor Groups are public
officials?

As suggested by Dianne Stone of your office, copies of the
County of San Diego Board of Supervisors Policies I-1 and I-1A are
enclosed, along with notations regarding the specific duties of the
groups that may be especially pertinent to your decision process.

If you have any questions regarding this matter, please call
Mr. James Miller or Mr. Phil Franklin at (619) 236-2675.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
January 24, 1992
Page Two

     We would appreciate your prompt action on this question.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        1991-92 SAN DIEGO COUNTY GRAND JURY

                                        [signature]
                                        RICHARD B. MACFIE, Foreman

                                        [signature]
                                        JAMES MILLER, Chair
                                        Environmental Management Committee

RBM/JM:jml

encs.(3)




(studies/eml)

Enclosure 1

     The San Diego County Board of Supervisors has established
planning and sponsor groups and mandated the purpose, functions,
and procedures for all such groups in Board of Supervisors'
Policies I-1 and I-1A. The Board of Supervisors has provided that:

     (1) Members of planning groups are elected in elections
conducted at public expense (page 2 of Policy I-1);

     (2) Members of planning groups must file financial
disclosure statements such as are required of public
officials (page 4 of Policy I-1);

     (3) Conduct and operation of the planning groups are
required to be governed by the Brown Act (page 5 of
Policy I-1);

     (4) County staff is made available to carry out work of
the planning and sponsor groups (page 8 of Policy I-1);

     (5) Public funds are budgeted to cover the work of the
planning and sponsor groups (page 8 of Policy I-1);

     (6) Though County staff may input recommendations, the
planning groups approve the final form and content of
development plans which they submit directly to the Board
of Supervisors (Plan Formulation, page 9 of Policy I-1);

     (7) Members of planning groups are mandated to disqualify
themselves from participating in actions on matters in
which they have conflicts of interest (Disqualification,
page 6 of Policy I-1);

     (8) Planning groups members may receive legal defense and
indemnification through the Office of County Counsel
(page 7 of Policy I-1 and page 1 of Policy I-1A).

     All of the foregoing provisions of Policy I-1 are consistent
with the planning and sponsor groups operating as public agencies
and with the members of the groups being considered public
officials.

     However, the Board of Supervisors has incorporated a provision
in Policy I-1 that is not consistent with the foregoing: that
members of planning and sponsor groups are not officers, employees,
or servants of the County of San Diego. This, in effect, exempts
them from jurisdiction of the FPPA and the Fair Political Practices
Commission (FPPC).
